coredata.dc:title: Adverse effect assessment of fluorescent carbon dots in cigarette smoke
coredata.dc:creator: Na, Xiaokang
Zhang, Lijuan
Wang, Haitao
Tan, Mingqian
coredata.dc:description: The potential adverse effects or health risks of nanostructures produced in smoking tobacco have drawn considerable attention. Herein, the presence of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) in cigarette smoke was confirmed, and their physicochemical property and potential toxicity were evaluated as well. The cigarette CDs were spherical and the size was mainly falling within 2–4 nm. Under ultraviolet excitation, the cigarette CDs emitted brilliant blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of 2.64%. Importantly, the cigarette CDs disturbed cell cycle and caused cell apoptosis in a dose-dependent manner. The metabolomics evaluation showed that the CDs resulted in the up-regulation of glycolysis, down-regulation of amino acids and metabolic disorder of lipids, demonstrating a significant metabolism shift in HepG2 cells upon exposure to the cigarette CDs. Our findings provided valuable information on the existence of CDs in cigarette smoke and their adverse effect on live cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cigarette smoke
Carbon dots
Metabolomics
Cytotoxicity
Tobacco

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of CDs from cigarette smoke
Bio-distribution of cigarette CDs in HepG2 cells
Presence of CDs in cigarette smoke
Properties of the CDs from cigarette smoke
Effect of cigarette CDs on metabolome and lipidome of HepG2 cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic diagram of the separation of CDs in cigarette smoke. (B) Photograph of cigarette CDs aqueous solution under day or ultraviolet lights. (C) TEM images of CDs from cigarette smoke (Scale bar, 50 nm), inset is the high-resolution TEM image (Scale bar, 5 nm). (D) The size distributions of CDs from cigarette smoke. (E) AFM topography 3D-image of the cigarette CDs on a mica substrate. (F) 2-dimensional AFM profile and (G) height along the line of cigarette CDs in AFM image.
(A) UV–visible absorption spectrum, (B) fluorescence spectra, (C) fluorescence decay and (D) FTIR spectra of the CDs from cigarette smoke.
(A) XPS survey and high resolution of (B) C1s, (C) O1s, and (D) N1s spectra of the CDs from cigarette smoke.

Apoptosis analysis by flow cytometry. (A) HepG2 cells without cigarette CDs treatment (negative control), or (B) treated with 0.2 mg mL−1 cigarette CDs, (C) 4 mg mL−1 cigarette CDs. (D) Relative proportion of cells treated with cigarette CDs at different concentrations. All experiments were performed in triplicates.
Heat map of 39 differential molecular species in HepG2 cells exposed to CDs as compared to the controls (t-test, p < 0.05).
Heat map of 182 differential lipid molecular species in HepG2 cells exposed to CDs as compared to the controls (t-test, p < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Adverse effect assessment of fluorescent carbon dots in cigarette smoke

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Technology, Dalian Polytechnic University, Qinggongyuan 1, Ganjingzi District, Dalian 116034, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Seafood

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The potential adverse effects or health risks of nanostructures produced in smoking tobacco have drawn considerable attention. Herein, the presence of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) in cigarette smoke was confirmed, and their physicochemical property and potential toxicity were evaluated as well. The cigarette CDs were spherical and the size was mainly falling within 2–4 nm. Under ultraviolet excitation, the cigarette CDs emitted brilliant blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of 2.64%. Importantly, the cigarette CDs disturbed cell cycle and caused cell apoptosis in a dose-dependent manner. The metabolomics evaluation showed that the CDs resulted in the up-regulation of glycolysis, down-regulation of amino acids and metabolic disorder of lipids, demonstrating a significant metabolism shift in HepG2 cells upon exposure to the cigarette CDs. Our findings provided valuable information on the existence of CDs in cigarette smoke and their adverse effect on live cells.

Fluorescent carbon dots in cigarette smoke lead to disorder of cellular metabolites.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Smoking has become one of the major hazards to human health worldwide. Long-term smoking results in a series of physical injuries, which increases the rate of morbidity and mortality, such as cancer (Gaudet et al., 2017; Islami et al., 2018), cardiovascular disease (Burns, 2003) and chronic kidney disease (Haroun et al., 2003). It is well known that the cigarette smoke contains complex mixture produced by burning, cracking and distillation of tobacco plants during smoking cigarettes (Baker et al., 2004). Although great effort has been made in identifying toxicity compounds in cigarette smoke and their toxicity mechanisms behind (Yauk et al., 2012), unknown substances, especially nanostructures that may cause adverse health effects (Kumar et al., 2013; Williams et al., 2013), remains ill-defined due to the sophistication of cigarette smoke. Therefore, the study of unknown nanostructures with potential adverse effects in cigarette smoke is still of great significance.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of fluorescent nanoparticles which are rich in carbon with spherical shape and a diameter usually less than 10 nm. Since their discovery in 2004 during preparing carbon nanotube (Xu et al., 2004), the CDs have been extensively studied and applied in various fields due to their unique physical and chemical properties (Lim et al., 2015). In recent years, the presence of CDs has also been found in many daily necessities (Wang et al., 2017; Li et al., 2018). Taking food items as an example, a series of complex reactions occurred between carbonaceous compounds in food materials during various thermal processes were believed as the major reason for the spontaneous formation of CDs (Wang et al., 2019). Similarly, it is reasonable to conjecture the carbonaceous compounds in tobacco will undergo complex carbonization at high temperatures during the burning process of cigarettes and result in formation of CDs. Recently, the existence of CDs in cigarette smoke was confirmed (Song et al., 2018). More importantly, it has been proved that some CDs are toxic to the cell at a certain dosage. For example, the CDs produced during the Maillard reaction could enter HepG2 cells and lead to cell death when their concentration was more than 5 mg mL−1 (Li et al., 2018) and those in beer cause cell cycle disturbances and apoptosis (Wang et al., 2019). Furthermore, the toxicity assessment of food-borne CDs suggested the toxicity of CDs was positively correlated to the thermal processing temperature. This indicated the CDs in cigarette smoke may lead to more serious adverse effects since they are derived from finely ground tobacco wrapped in paper and the temperature during cigarette burning is much higher than that in food processing. However, there is no information about the CDs from cigarette smoke, regardless of their properties, biodistribution, or potential toxicity.

Metabolomics is a powerful tool that has been used to evaluate the metabolite changes in complex biological samples. In recent years, the development of metabolomics has greatly enhanced their ability to analyze the metabolism response of organisms under various conditions (Wang et al., 2018). Specific to nanomaterials, metabolomics method has been widely used to reveal the metabolism changes of plants after exposure to nanoparticles, which revealed that nanoparticles, such as CdS nanoparticles and silver nanoparticles can induce changes in plant metabolism (H.L. Zhang et al., 2018, J.L. Zhang et al., 2018, Tian et al., 2020). These effects were mainly reflected in the amino acid metabolism, phospholipid metabolism, energy metabolism, and activation of the antioxidant defense system. The metabolomics method was also used to reveal changes of phosphorylcholine-containing lipids in rat lungs after exposure to ZnO nanoparticles (Lee et al., 2018). For food items, recent research revealed that the CDs extracted from roast salmon induced metabolism changes for lipid and glucose metabolism (Song et al., 2020). Therefore, it is desired to evaluate the effects of CD derived from cigarette smoke on cellular metabolism in revealing their potential toxicity.

This work confirmed the existence of CDs in cigarette smoke and reported the purification method of the cigarette CDs. The ultraviolet and visible (UV–Vis) absorption and fluorescence properties of the CDs derived from cigarette smoke were characterized. The size and morphology of the CDs were studied by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM). The cell distribution of cigarette CDs and their effects on cell cycle, apoptosis, and overall metabolic level in HepG2 cells were studied using fluorescence imaging, flow cytometry and metabolomics methods. These results helped us to reveal the composition of cigarette smoke and the potential risks of cigarette CDs.

Zhongnanhai cigarette was purchased from a local market (Dalian, China). Quinine sulfate, isopropanol, ammonia, and acetonitrile were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). HepG2 cell line was purchased from the Cell Bank of Type Culture Collection of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum and DMEM medium were purchased from Hyclone Laboratories (Logan, UT, USA). Ammonium acetate was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of CDs from cigarette smoke
ce:section.ce:para: Cigarette smoke was collected using distilled water as a filter as shown in Fig. 1A. The aqueous phase that contained CDs was concentrated by rotary evaporator and further purified through preparative chromatograph equipped with a C18 column (methanol: water = 9:1; flow rate = 20 mL min−1). The fractions with fluorescence emission were collected and further concentrated. The purified CDs from cigarette smoke were lyophilized and stored in a desiccator for further assessment.

The morphology, size distribution, surface groups and optical properties of the CDs from cigarette smoke were examined according to previously reported methods (Li et al., 2018). The purity of CDs obtained from cigarette smoke was measured using nicotine as an indicator. The nicotine was determined by a gas chromatography-mass spectrometer (Agilent 7890B) with a DB-5 capillary column (length: 20 m; internal diameter: 0.2 mm; film thickness 0.33 μm). The carrier gas was helium (99.999%). The temperature of the injection port was 240 °C and the split ratio was 1:50. The initial oven temperature was 100 °C and held for 1 min, then the temperature raised at a rate of 30 °C min−1 and maintained at 260 °C for 5 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-distribution of cigarette CDs in HepG2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: HepG2 cells were incubated with cigarette CDs for 3 h at a concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1, thereafter, the medium that contained CDs was removed and the potential residue was removed by PBS washing. The images of the HepG2 cells were captured by a laser scanning confocal microscope (SP8, Leica, Wetzlar, Germany). The wavelengths of 408, 458, and 488 nm were used to excite the fluorescence of CDs.

The effects of cigarette CDs on cell cycle and apoptosis were evaluated by propidium iodide staining and annexin V-FITC/PI staining, respectively. Both cell cycle and apoptosis kits were purchased from Beyotime (Shanghai, China). The HepG2 cells in the logarithmic phase of growth were seeded in a 6-well plate at a density of 105 cells mL−1. After attachment, the cells were treated with cigarette CDs at a concentration of 0.2 and 2 mg mL−1 for 5 h. For cell cycle analysis, the collected cells were fixed with 70% ethanol for 12 h at 4 °C, followed by staining with propidium iodide. For apoptosis analysis, the cell pellet was washed with PBS buffer for three times and re-suspended in the binding buffer before the addition of annexin V-FITC and propidium iodide. The fluorescence was detected by a flow cytometer (BD, Franklin Lakes, USA).

The HepG2 cells were seeded in 6-well plate at about 2 × 105 cells per well. After treatment of 2 mg mL−1 cigarette CDs for 5 h, the cell pellet was collected for lipid extraction after adding lipid standard phosphatidylcholine (PC) 17:0/17:0, phosphatidylethanolamine (PE) 17:0/17:0, triacylglycerol (TG) 17:0/17:0/17:0, phosphatidylglycerol (PG) 17:0/17:0 and phosphatidylinositol (PI) 18:0/18:0). Lipid was extracted by methanol and chloroform. Briefly, 300 μL mixture of methanol and chloroform (methanol: chloroform = 2:1) were added to cell pellet and vortex for 1 min, then, 300 μL H2O was added. The chloroform phase was collected after being centrifuged at 10,000g for 15 min at 4 °C. The chloroform was removed by nitrogen and the extracted lipids were stored at −80 °C. A reversed-phase BEH C8 column (2.1 mm × 100 mm, 1.7 μm, Waters) was used for the chromatographic separation of lipids. Parameters of the ultra-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometer were followed the previous paper (Xuan et al., 2018). Quality control samples were prepared by pooling equal aliquots of all cell samples.

The HepG2 cells were incubated with cigarette CDs for 5 h at a concentration of 2 mg mL−1, and the medium was replaced with fresh medium and cultured for 2 h before metabolite extraction. The metabolite extraction procedure was modified according to the previous report (Yuan et al., 2012). Briefly, the plate was placed on dry ice, 2 μL of internal standard (L-phenylalanine, undecanoic acid, and nonadecanoic acid, 5 μg mL−1) and 4 mL of pre-cooled 80% aqueous methanol were added to each well. After incubation for 20 min at −80 °C, the cells were collected by a cell scraper and the cell debris were removed by centrifuge. The supernatant that contained cell metabolites was lyophilized and stored at −80 °C for further analysis. An amide XBridge HPLC column (4.6 mm × 100 mm, 3.5 μm, Waters) was used for the chromatographic separation of metabolites.

The results were expressed as means ± standard deviation (SD), which were analyzed by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). Statistical product and service solutions (SPSS) statistics 19 package (IBM, USA) were used for statistical analysis. Statistically significant differences were reported if p < 0.05. Internal standards were used to quantify the relative abundance of metabolites. The R programming language (version 3.6.3) were employed to perform t-test, both p and false discovery rate (FDR) values of <0.05 were considered significantly different from the control. The heatmap was obtained by software TBtools 6.2. Metabolic pathway analysis was obtained by MetaboAnalyst 4.0 (https://www.metaboanalyst.ca).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Presence of CDs in cigarette smoke
ce:section.ce:para: The device used to capture CDs in cigarette smoke is shown in Fig. 1A. The cigarette smoke was blown to go through deionized water by an air blower. The water-insoluble components of smoke were discarded as exhaust gas, while the water-soluble CDs were collected in water. The residual tar and solid particles were removed by extraction steps. To eliminate the influence of nicotine, one of the most toxic substances in cigarette smoke that adhered on CDs, preparative chromatography was used to purify the CDs in aqueous solution, and the effluent fractions with fluorescence were collected (Fig. S2). The nicotine content in CDs was detected by gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) before and after purification (Fig. S3), and negligible nicotine was detected in CDs after purification by preparative chromatography to ensure the reliability of results from subsequent experimental results of CDs. The color of purified CDs aqueous solution was pale yellow under visible light and emitted bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet (UV) excitation (Fig. 1B). This phenomenon provided primary evidence of the presence of CDs in cigarette smoke. The TEM image displayed in Fig. 1C further demonstrated that our hypothesis was true and the CDs were present in cigarette smoke. The organic molecules could not be observed by TEM due to their low contrast. The CDs separated from cigarette smoke were subsphaeroidal with size in the range of 2.0–4.5 nm and their average diameter was about 2.8 nm (Fig. 1D). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of a single dot (Fig. 1C, inset) indicated that the lattice size of CDs was about 0.18 nm, which was in line with the [102] facet of graphitic carbon (Sahu et al., 2012). The morphology of the CDs was further investigated by atomic force microscopy (Fig. 1E). The AFM results suggested that the CDs were monodispersed and their topographic height was lower than 5 nm (Fig. 1F, G), which was in good agreement with the TEM characterization. The formation of CDs in cigarette smoke could be attributed to the burning process of tobacco leaves and cigarette paper. To confirm the major source of cigarette CDs, the smoke from tobacco leaves and cigarette paper were collected separately (Fig. S1A). As expected, the presence of CDs in cigarette smoke from tobacco leaves and cigarette paper was demonstrated by TEM (Fig. S1B, C). Furthermore, the optical properties of CDs from different parts of cigarettes were characterized. The UV–vis absorption of CDs from cigarette paper was weak than those from tobacco leaves, which indicated the structure difference between the two kinds of CDs. The fluorescence spectra indicated the fluorescence behavior of CDs from cigarette paper and tobacco leaves was similar to CDs collected from smoke produced by burning a whole cigarette (Fig. S1E, F). Moreover, the yield of CDs from different parts of cigarettes was calculated. As for a single cigarette, 5.22 mg of CDs were produced from tobacco leaves while about 0.68 mg CDs were from the cigarette paper. Thus, the major source of CDs from cigarettes was tobacco leaves.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of the CDs from cigarette smoke
ce:section.ce:para: Optical properties of CDs from cigarette smoke were studied by UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the cigarette CDs (Fig. 2A) shows a single peak at 325 nm, which can be attributed to the n-π* electronic transition of CO (Du et al., 2015). The fluorescence emission peaks of CDs from cigarette smoke shifted to a longer wavelength with the increase of excitation wavelength. This typical fluorescence emission behavior was related to surface defects and functional groups of CDs, which was also reported in previous work (Zhu et al., 2015). The bathochromic emission provided additional evidence about the presence of CDs in cigarette smoke. The maximum excitation wavelength of 360 nm corresponded to the maximum emission wavelength of 455 nm (Fig. 2B). Using quinine sulfate as a standard, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs from cigarette smoke was calculated to be 2.64% (Table S1). The fluorescence decay curve of CDs dispersed in water at room temperature was nonmonoexponential, and the average fluorescence lifetime was 7.49 ns (χ2 = 1.136) (Fig. 2C).

The surface chemical groups of CDs were characterized by FTIR spectra (Fig. 2D). The peak centered at 3392 cm−1 was attributed to the absorption band of the amino and hydroxyl groups. The peaks near 2952 cm−1 were caused by CH vibration. The strong peak at 1040 cm−1 indicated the presence of CO. Besides, peak at around 1592 cm−1 could be due to the stretching vibration of CO while the absorption band at 1385 cm−1 was assigned to symmetric carboxylate stretching. The results from FTIR spectra indicated that the methylene, amino, carboxylate, and hydroxyl groups were existing on the CDs surface.

The elemental analysis of CDs from cigarette smoke was performed by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and there were three major peaks in the XPS spectra. The peaks at 285, 400, and 532 eV were assigned to carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, respectively (Fig. 3A). The relative contents of three elements were calculated to be 67.22%, 8.31% and 23.76% through integral the peak area (Wang et al., 2012). Notably, the higher carbon content of CDs may be attributed to the high carbonization of tobacco at high temperature. The chemical state on the CDs surface was analyzed through high-resolution XPS spectra. The peaks at 284.6, 285.4, 286.4 and 287.6 eV in high-resolution C1s XPS spectra (Fig. 3B) correspond to C-C/C=C, CN, CO and CO groups, respectively (Chen et al., 2014; Jiang et al., 2015). The high-resolution O1s XPS spectra (Fig. 3C) demonstrated the presence of OH, *O=C-O, and CO groups since their characteristic peaks were observed at 530.7, 531.4, and 532.3 eV (Song et al., 2016). Besides, the high-resolution N1s XPS spectra (Fig. 3D) could be decomposed into two peaks at 399.3 and 400.8 eV, which were assigned to pyridinic N and NH, respectively (Chen et al., 2014). Thus, the results of XPS suggested the presence of amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl on the cigarette CDs surface, which were highly consistent with the results obtained from FTIR spectra.

Materials in nanosize exhibit distinct physicochemical properties in contrast to the corresponding bulk materials. Previous results have revealed that nanomaterials could penetrate the cellular membrane and disturb normal cell metabolism. Therefore, the in vitro distribution and cytotoxicity of CDs from cigarette smoke was investigated. Cellular distribution of CDs was evaluated by laser confocal microscopy after incubation with HepG2 cells. As shown in Fig. S4B, the HepG2 cells emitted strong blue fluorescence when excited by 405 nm laser after incubation with cigarette CDs for 3 h. Similarly, green and red fluorescence was also observed when excited at 455 nm (Fig. S4C) and 488 nm (Fig. S4D, very weak), respectively. The fluorescence intensity weakened under a longer excitation wavelength due to the bathochromic emission property. Notably, the fluorescence was mainly enriched in the cytoplasm area while almost no fluorescence was observed in the nucleus region. Those results suggested that the cigarette CDs could be uptaken by HepG2 cells and accumulated in cytoplasm other than the nucleus. According to previous reports, CDs may induce apoptosis or necrosis of the cells after entering cells (Wang et al., 2019). For HepG2 cells, the cigarette CDs were a foreign substance, and their impact on cells was particularly important. Therefore, the effect of cigarette CDs on cell viability was analyzed by the MTT assay. The result indicated that the cell viability was decreased in a concentration-dependent manner after being treated with cigarette CDs for 12 h, and the survival rate at a concentration of 2 mg∙mL−1 was about 50% (Fig. S5). In addition, the cytotoxicity of cigarette CDs was further analyzed by flow cytometry after incubation with cigarette CDs at concentrations of 0.2 and 2 mg mL−1 for 5 h (Fig. 4 ). There was no significant apoptosis in HepG2 cells incubated with lower concentrations of cigarette CDs (0.2 mg∙mL−1), while the decrease in the relative number of living cells suggested that the cell viability was affected by low concentration cigarette CDs although at a relatively low extent (Fig. 4B, D). In contrast, the relative number of viable cells was significantly reduced after treatment with high dose cigarette CDs (2 mg∙mL−1), correspondingly, the relative number of apoptotic cells increased as well (Fig. 4C, D). The results provided additional evidence that the cigarette CDs reduced cell viability in a dose-dependent manner. The cell cycle was a series of events that happen during cell division. To investigate whether CDs from cigarette smoke affect the cell cycle of HepG2 cells, the cell cycle was analyzed after incubation with cigarette smoke for 5 h at concentrations of 0.2 and 2 mg∙mL−1, respectively. As shown in Fig. S6, the proportion of cells in both S phase and G2/M phase were unaffected by cigarette CDs. In contrast, the proportion of cells in the G1/G0 phase decreased from 60% to 55% in the presence of cigarette CDs. In addition, the cigarette CDs also caused a significant increase of cells in the Sub-G1 phase. When the CDs concentration was 2 mg∙mL−1, the proportion of Sub-G1 phase cells increased from 0.65% to 10%. This result suggested that the cigarette CDs led to cell apoptosis which was in agreement with the results from cell apoptosis assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of cigarette CDs on metabolome and lipidome of HepG2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain an in-depth understanding of the mechanism of cigarette CD toxicity, the metabolite profile in the cigarette-treated cells was investigated. Unsupervised principal component analysis (PCA) and partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) were performed to verify the repeatability of the method used for metabolites detection. As shown in Figs. S7 and S8, the quality control samples were tightly clustered and located in the central of score plots, which demonstrated the stability and repeatability of the LC-MS platform. Furthermore, the cigarette CD-treated cells were separated from control cells in both PCA and PLS-DA score plots, suggesting that the cigarette CDs induced significant variation in intracellular metabolites.

Fig. 5 shows that a total of 39 metabolites were significantly altered in HepG2 cells in CD-treated groups compared with those in control groups by the metabolomics. The pathway analysis of the cigarette CD disturbance using MetaboAnalyst revealed that amino acid metabolism, glycolysis, pyrimidine metabolism, and nicotinate metabolism were the main metabolic pathways affected by cigarette CDs (Fig. S9). The dihydroxyacetone phosphate and phosphoenolpyruvate were involved in glycolysis, which increased 4.0 and 2.2-fold, respectively, after incubation with the cigarette CDs. In normal cells, the energy was mainly generated by the respiratory chain in the mitochondria. The obvious decrease in the ATP level demonstrated that energy production was disrupted by the cigarette CDs. Therefore, the alternative pathway was needed to compensate for energy production. The increased level of intermediate metabolites in glycolysis indicated the upregulation of glycolysis. The glycolysis may serve as a complemental route for energy production after exposure to cigarette CDs (Yan et al., 2019).

The purine metabolism was affected by cigarette CDs as well. The metabolites involved in purine metabolisms, such as adenosine, inosine, guanosine, and hypoxanthine, were all up-regulated in cigarette CD-treated groups compared with those in control groups. This suggested that the nucleic acid degradation was significantly increased and perturbation of purine metabolism occurred after exposure to cigarette CDs. Hydrogen peroxide, a strong oxidant, could be produced through the catalysis of xanthine oxidase. It should be noted that the hypoxanthine was the substrates of xanthine oxidase, the evaluation of the hypoxanthine level as well as the upregulation of the nucleic acid degradation pathway might result in production of excess hydrogen peroxide which led to oxidative stress (Zhang et al., 2019). Compared with the control group, all detected amino acids in cigarette CD-treated groups were significantly down-regulated. Since amino acids are building blocks of proteins, the low amino acid level might affect protein biosynthesis. Furthermore, amino acids involved in the urea cycle, such as glutamine, glutamate, and aspartate were decreased, indicating the urea cycle were down-regulated too (Geng et al., 2015).

Lipids play an important role in cell membrane formation, energy storage, signal transmission, and material transportation. To further investigate whether the cigarette CDs have an effect on lipid metabolism in HepG2 cells, lipid change in cells before and after cigarette CDs treatment was analyzed by lipidomics strategy. A total of 182 lipids species were significantly altered, among which, 124 lipid species were down-regulated and 58 lipid species were up-regulated (Fig. 6 ), including diacylglycerols (DGs), triacylglycerols (TGs), sphingomyelins (SM), phosphatidylethanolamines (PE), alkyl and alkenyl substituent PEs (PE-O), ceramides (Cer), phosphatidylcholine (PC), and alkyl and alkenyl substituent PCs (PC-O). This result indicated that lipid metabolism was disrupted by cigarette CDs. The SM showed a decreasing tendency after cigarette CDs exposure, on the contrary, the important second messengers Cer exhibited an increasing trend in the same condition, which are generated via hydrolysis of SMs. The accumulation of Cer in cell was associated with apoptosis. The results obtained from lipidomics further demonstrated the reason of cigarette CDs induced cell apoptosis (Fig. 4). Moreover, the alkyl and alkenyl substituent PEs (PE-O) were significantly decreased in cigarette CDs treatment groups. The previous report revealed that the phospholipid with vinyl-ether bond played important roles in maintaining the normal physiological function, especially in free radical scavenging (Broniec et al., 2011). Therefore, the decrease of PE-O suggested that cigarette CDs induced oxidative stress in HepG2 cells. It is noteworthy that almost all significantly changed PC-O (83%) were upregulated. Comparted with PE-O, the number of double bonds in PC-O was less than that in PE-O. A lower degree of unsaturation made PC-O less sensitive to free radicals and might accumulate to compensate for the decrease of PE-O. The PCs composition exhibited an obvious variation that was related to maintaining the cell membrane under environmental stress. Overall, the PCs were enhanced after being treated with cigarette CDs although some PC species were decreased. Previously, the metabolomics results from the smoker's serum also suggested the increased tendency of PCs (Wang-Sattler et al., 2008). These results revealed that the enhanced PCs synthesis might alleviate cigarette CDs induced adverse health effects. The TG served as fatty acid buffers to store excessive fatty acid or provide acyl groups for phospholipid synthesis. In this study, the most significantly altered TG species (more than 80%) were down-regulated after treatment with cigarette CDs, suggesting an enhanced breakdown of TGs. The DGs were the direct precursors for TGs synthesis. Cigarette CDs exposure led to an obvious decrease tendency in DGs, which further demonstrated the decline in TGs. Since PCs content increased after cigarette CDs treatment, the acyl groups in TGs might be used for PCs synthesis. In addition, the DGs were an important component in cell membranes and participate in regulating endocytosis and exocytosis. Down-regulation of DGs might directly affect cytoskeletal reorganization, membrane transport, and endocytosis, which led to metabolic disorders (Almena and Merida, 2011).

In summary, the existence of CDs in cigarette smoke was reported in this study. The cigarette CDs were isolated and purified from cigarette smoke, and their properties were characterized. The results revealed that the CDs in cigarette smoke were spherical particles with a narrow size distribution, which could easily enter HepG2 cells, affect cell cycle progression, and induce apoptosis. Besides, the cigarette CDs led to lipid metabolism disorder, and the DGs, TGs, PEs, PE-Os, and SMs were down-regulated while the PCs and Cer were up-regulated. Furthermore, the cigarette CDs affected glycolysis, amino acid metabolism, and pyrimidine metabolism of HepG2 cells as well. This work demonstrated a novel nanostructure present in cigarette smoke and provided a potential risk assessment for cigarette CDs.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Xiaokang Na: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Lijuan Zhang: Visualization, Data curation. Haitao Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Mingqian Tan: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Central Funds Guiding the Local Science and Technology Development (2020JH6/10500002), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31872915) and the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFC1600702).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary material: Quantum yield; TEM image, ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra, and fluorescence spectra of CDs collected from smoke produced by burning cigarette paper and tobacco leaves; Preparative chromatography; Gas chromatography; in vitro distribution; Cell viability; Cell cycle analysis; PCA and PLS-DA score plot; Pathway analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.impact.2020.100241.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Almena and Merida, 2011

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Shaping up the membrane: diacylglycerol coordinates spatial orientation of signaling

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Almena, M. and I. Merida (2011). Shaping up the membrane: diacylglycerol coordinates spatial orientation of signaling. Trends in Biochemical Sciences 36(11): 593-603. 10.1016/j.tibs.2011.06.005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An overview of the effects of tobacco ingredients on smoke chemistry and toxicity
